---
title: "Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands"
subtitle: "Day 10 — Quantitative biology"
author: "Quantitative biology and integrative omics"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    highlight-style: github
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-line-numbers: false
    incremental: false
    chalkboard: false
    menu: true
---

## About this lecture

This is the **final day** of the linear-algebra block. Yesterday you built HMMs
by hand; today we let R do the heavy lifting on genome-like data. By the end
you will be able to:

- Build an HMM in R with the CRAN [`HMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM)
  package via `initHMM` (states, symbols, $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, $A$, $B$).
- **Simulate** sequence with `simHMM` and recover its likelihood with
  `forward`.
- **Decode** the most likely hidden-state path with `viterbi` and read it as a
  predicted **CpG-island map**.
- **Learn** the parameters $A$, $B$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ from *unlabelled*
  sequence with `baumWelch`.
- Discuss **how many states** to use and the main **pitfalls**.

Notation as Day 9: transition matrix $A$, emission matrix $B$, initial
distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, hidden states $\mathbf{z}$, observations
$\mathbf{x}$.

::: notes
Welcome to day ten, the last day of the block. Yesterday was all concept and
pen-and-paper; today is hands-on R. We use the same two-state GC-rich versus
normal model so nothing about the biology is new — only the tooling. The HMM
package gives us one function per task: build, simulate, score, decode, and
learn. Code on these slides is illustrative, not executable; the exercise is
where you run it.
:::

## Recap and today's tool {.smaller}

Day 9: an HMM = hidden states + transition matrix $A$ + emission matrix $B$ +
initial distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, with three problems —
**likelihood** (forward), **decoding** (Viterbi), **learning** (Baum–Welch).

The CRAN [`HMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) package gives one
function for each:

| Task (Day 9) | `HMM` function |
|--|--|
| build the model | `initHMM` |
| generate data | `simHMM` |
| likelihood $P(\mathbf{x})$ | `forward` (and `backward`) |
| decode best path $\mathbf{z}^\*$ | `viterbi` |
| learn $A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi}$ | `baumWelch` |

```r
install.packages("HMM")   # once
library(HMM)
```

::: notes
Map each Day-9 concept onto exactly one function so the package feels like a
direct translation of yesterday's ideas. initHMM builds the four ingredients,
simHMM runs the generative story, forward and backward give the likelihood,
viterbi decodes, and baumWelch learns. Mention depmixS4 as a more flexible
alternative for richer emission models, but we stick with HMM because it maps
cleanly onto the concepts.
:::

## Building the model with `initHMM` {.smaller}

The same 2-state model as Day 9. States $H$ (GC-rich), $L$ (normal); symbols
the bases.

```r
library(HMM)

states  <- c("H", "L")
symbols <- c("A", "C", "G", "T")

startProbs <- c(0.5, 0.5)                    # pi

transProbs <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2,             # A: rows = from, cols = to
                       0.3, 0.7),
                     nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(states, states))

emissionProbs <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1,  # B: rows = state, cols = base
                          0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3),
                        nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                        dimnames = list(states, symbols))

hmm <- initHMM(States = states, Symbols = symbols,
               startProbs = startProbs,
               transProbs = transProbs,
               emissionProbs = emissionProbs)
```

Each matrix row must **sum to 1** — exactly the constraint from Day 9.

::: notes
Type this slowly. The arguments to initHMM are the four ingredients by name:
States and Symbols are the labels, startProbs is pi, transProbs is A, and
emissionProbs is B. Use byrow equals TRUE so the matrices read like the page,
and dimnames so rows and columns are labelled. Stress the row-sum-to-one
constraint; a common bug is a typo that makes a row sum to 0.99 or 1.01.
:::

## Simulating sequence with `simHMM` {.smaller}

`simHMM` runs the generative story for you and, because it is a simulation, also
hands back the **true** hidden states — a luxury you never get with real data,
but perfect for testing.

```r
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 30)

sim$observation   # the emitted bases  (this is your "data")
#  [1] "G" "C" "C" "G" "A" ...

sim$states        # the TRUE hidden path (normally unknown!)
#  [1] "H" "H" "H" "H" "L" ...
```

We will pretend we only saw `sim$observation`, then check whether decoding
recovers `sim$states`.

::: notes
simHMM is our test harness. It samples a hidden path from the chain and emits a
base at each step, returning both. The observation vector is what we would get
from a sequencer; the states vector is the ground truth that real biology hides
from us. The plan is honest model evaluation: hide the truth, run the
algorithms on the observations alone, then compare against the truth we
secretly kept.
:::

## Likelihood with `forward` {.smaller}

`forward` returns a matrix of **log** forward probabilities, states × position.
The likelihood $P(\mathbf{x})$ is recovered from the **last column**.

```r
obs <- sim$observation

f <- forward(hmm, obs)        # log alpha values, rows = states, cols = positions

# total log-likelihood = log-sum-exp of the final column
logLik <- function(col) {
  m <- max(col)
  m + log(sum(exp(col - m)))
}
logLik(f[, length(obs)])
# e.g.  -39.5      (a log-probability: the raw probability is astronomically tiny)
```

Probabilities of long sequences are vanishingly small, so the package works in
**logs** — this is why your by-hand products from Day 9 shrank so fast.

::: notes
forward gives the log alpha matrix. To get the data log-likelihood you combine
the final column with a numerically safe log-sum-exp — subtract the max before
exponentiating to avoid underflow. Connect this back to yesterday: when they
multiplied six probabilities they already saw the number collapse toward zero;
over thousands of bases it underflows entirely, which is exactly why everything
is done in log space. They mainly need to know that forward gives likelihood and
that it lives in logs.
:::

## Decoding with `viterbi`: the island map {.smaller}

`viterbi` returns the single most likely hidden path — one label per base.

```r
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)
path
#  [1] "H" "H" "H" "H" "L" "L" "L" "H" "H" ...

# How well did we recover the truth?
mean(path == sim$states)      # fraction of positions correctly decoded, e.g. 0.87

# Read off predicted CpG islands: maximal runs of "H"
rle(path)
# Run Length Encoding
#   lengths: int [1:5] 4 3 2 ...
#   values : chr [1:5] "H" "L" "H" ...
```

The **runs of `H`** are your **predicted CpG islands**.
[`rle`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/rle.html) turns the path into segments.

::: notes
This is the biological payoff. viterbi takes only the observations and returns
the best hidden label per position. Compare to sim states to measure accuracy;
it will not be perfect because the emissions are genuinely ambiguous, but it
should be high. Then rle compresses the path into runs, and each run of H is a
predicted island with a start, end, and length. This is a real genome
annotation produced from raw sequence.
:::

## Learning with `baumWelch` {.smaller}

So far we **supplied** $A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi}$. The realistic case: only raw
sequence, no labels. `baumWelch` learns the parameters by EM.

```r
# start from a deliberately WRONG guess to show it can learn
guess <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                 transProbs    = matrix(c(0.5,0.5, 0.5,0.5), 2, byrow=TRUE,
                                        dimnames=list(states,states)),
                 emissionProbs = matrix(rep(0.25, 8), 2, byrow=TRUE,
                                        dimnames=list(states,symbols)))

bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)

bw$hmm$transProbs      # learned A  -> should drift toward 0.8/0.2, 0.3/0.7
bw$hmm$emissionProbs   # learned B  -> H row should become GC-heavy
```

`baumWelch` returns `$hmm` (the fitted model) and `$difference` (convergence
trace).

::: notes
Baum-Welch is the learning step. We hand it a deliberately uninformative
starting model — uniform transitions and uniform emissions — and let it iterate.
With enough data it pulls the parameters toward the truth: the H row of the
emission matrix becomes GC-heavy and the transitions become sticky. Point out
the return structure: dollar hmm is the fitted model you then feed to viterbi,
and dollar difference lets you check it converged. Then immediately raise the
caveats on the next slide.
:::

## Pitfalls of learning {.smaller}

`baumWelch` is powerful but easy to misuse:

- **Local optima.** EM climbs to the *nearest* peak, not the global one.
  Different starting guesses → different fits. **Run several inits**, keep the
  best likelihood.
- **Label switching.** Which learned state is "H" is arbitrary — the algorithm
  may swap them. Interpret by the **emission profile** (the GC-heavy one is your
  island state), not by the name.
- **Needs enough data.** Short sequences give unstable estimates.
- **Garbage in, garbage out.** The fitted states are just clusters; they are
  "CpG islands" only if you **check** their emission profile makes biological
  sense.

::: notes
Spend real time here; this is where students get burned. EM only finds a local
optimum, so multiple restarts and picking the best log-likelihood is standard
practice. Label switching trips everyone up: the state the package calls H need
not be the GC-rich one, so always read the learned emission matrix to decide
which state means island. More data stabilises estimates. And finally, an HMM
will happily partition any sequence into states whether or not they mean
anything biological — interpretation is on you.
:::

## How many hidden states? {.smaller}

We assumed **2** states (island vs not). In general you must choose the number.

- Let **biology** guide you first: island vs background is naturally 2; coding
  / intron / intergenic might want 3+.
- More states **always** fit the training data better (higher likelihood) — so
  raw likelihood **cannot** pick the number. Penalise complexity with
  **AIC/BIC**, or use **held-out** sequence.
- More states = more parameters = more data needed and more local optima.

Start simple, add states only if they earn their keep and stay interpretable.

::: notes
Choosing the number of states is a model-selection problem, the same tension we
will meet repeatedly later in the course. Biology gives the first guess. Warn
them that likelihood always rises with more states, so you cannot choose by
likelihood alone; you need a penalty like AIC or BIC, or validation on held-out
data. And every extra state costs parameters, data, and stability. The mantra:
start simple, justify any added complexity.
:::

## From toy to real genomes {.smaller}

What changes when you move from `simHMM` data to a real chromosome:

- **Symbols.** Use the 4 bases, or richer features (dinucleotides like `CpG`,
  or k-mers) for sharper islands.
- **Scale.** Real sequences are megabases long; `forward`/`viterbi` are linear
  in length, so this is fine — but **work in logs** (the package does).
- **Validation.** Compare decoded islands against annotated CpG islands
  (e.g. UCSC tracks) to judge the model.
- **Beyond 2 states.** Gene-finding HMMs (e.g. GENSCAN-style) use many states
  for exons, introns, splice sites — same machinery, bigger $A$ and $B$.

::: notes
Bridge from the toy to practice. The biggest real-world refinement is the
emission alphabet: modelling dinucleotides captures CpG depletion far better
than single bases. Reassure them that scale is not a problem because the
algorithms are linear in sequence length. Stress validation against known
annotations, and note that serious gene finders are just large HMMs with the
identical forward, Viterbi, and Baum-Welch underneath.
:::

## Summary {.smaller}

- The CRAN [`HMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) package = one
  function per Day-9 concept: `initHMM`, `simHMM`, `forward`, `viterbi`,
  `baumWelch`.
- **Decode** with `viterbi`; the **runs of the GC-rich state are your predicted
  CpG islands**.
- **Learn** parameters from unlabelled sequence with `baumWelch` — but watch
  for **local optima**, **label switching**, and **enough data**.
- Choosing the **number of states** is model selection: let biology lead, use
  AIC/BIC or held-out data, keep states interpretable.

That closes the linear-algebra block — from solving systems to chains and
hidden chains.

::: notes
Recap the function-per-concept mapping and the biological read-out of the
decoded path. Re-emphasise the three pitfalls of learning and the model-
selection nature of choosing the number of states. This is the end of the whole
linear-algebra arc; the next slide points forward to where these ideas resurface
in the rest of the course.
:::

## Where this leads

This is the **end of the linear-algebra block**. The thinking carries forward:

- **Week 2 — PCA & applications.** From discrete hidden states to *continuous*
  latent directions: PCA finds the axes (eigenvectors!) that explain variation
  in omics data — the eigen-ideas from days 5–6 return.
- **Bayesian statistics.** HMMs were our first **latent-variable** models with
  proper inference; the Bayesian block generalises "infer the hidden given the
  observed" with priors and full posteriors.
- **Single-cell analysis.** Segmenting and annotating cells echoes today's
  segmenting and annotating a genome.

You now have the full toolkit — linear systems, vectors, matrices, eigenvalues,
Markov chains, and HMMs — that the rest of the course builds on.

::: notes
End by pointing forward, not back. PCA is the natural next step: continuous
latent structure found via eigenvectors, tying back to days five and six. The
Bayesian block generalises today's infer-the-hidden idea with priors and
posteriors. And single-cell annotation rhymes with island annotation. Congratulate
them — they have crossed the hardest block, and everything after this leans on
it.
:::
